description.en: Learn to turn sentences into vectors with the lightweight all-MiniLM-L6-v2 model and build a working semantic search engine â€” the embedding backbone of every RAG system.

story_variants.en:
## The Day Words Became Numbers

Vaathiyaar leans back and smiles. "Students, here is a riddle. The sentences *'How do I reset my password?'* and *'I forgot my login credentials'* share almost no words â€” yet they mean nearly the same thing. How does a machine know? Keyword search would fail miserably here. Today I teach you the trick that powers ChatGPT's memory, every RAG pipeline, and Homie's own brain: **embeddings**."

### What Is an Embedding?

An embedding is a list of numbers â€” a *vector* â€” that captures the *meaning* of a piece of text. Similar meanings land close together in this high-dimensional space; unrelated ones drift far apart. The model `sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2` maps any sentence to a **384-dimensional vector**, and it does so fast enough to run on a laptop CPU.

```python
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer

model = SentenceTransformer('all-MiniLM-L6-v2')
vec = model.encode("How do I reset my password?")
print(vec.shape)  # (384,)
```

### Why This Model?

Vaathiyaar pulls up a comparison on the board.

| Model | Dimensions | Size | Speed (CPU) | Best For |
|-------|-----------|------|-------------|----------|
| all-MiniLM-L6-v2 | 384 | ~80 MB | Very fast | Local RAG, prototypes |
| all-mpnet-base-v2 | 768 | ~420 MB | Slower | Higher accuracy |
| OpenAI text-embedding-3 | 1536 | API only | Network-bound | Cloud apps |

"MiniLM is the people's champion," he says. "Tiny, free, offline, and *good enough* for almost everything. That is why it is Homie's default local embedding model."

### Measuring Similarity

Once text is a vector, *meaning* becomes *geometry*. We compare two vectors with **cosine similarity** â€” the cosine of the angle between them. A score near `1.0` means "basically identical meaning"; near `0.0` means "unrelated."

```python
from sentence_transformers import util

a = model.encode("How do I reset my password?", convert_to_tensor=True)
b = model.encode("I forgot my login credentials", convert_to_tensor=True)
print(util.cos_sim(a, b))  # tensor([[0.71]])
```

### Building a Mini Semantic Search

This is the heart of RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation): embed a knowledge base once, then for every question embed the query and retrieve the closest passages.

```python
docs = [
    "Reset your password from the Settings page.",
    "Our office opens at 9 AM on weekdays.",
    "Contact billing for refund requests.",
]
doc_emb = model.encode(docs, convert_to_tensor=True)

query = model.encode("I can't log in", convert_to_tensor=True)
hits = util.semantic_search(query, doc_emb, top_k=1)
print(docs[hits[0][0]['corpus_id']])
# -> Reset your password from the Settings page.
```

Notice the magic: the query "I can't log in" matched the password document *despite zero shared keywords*. That is semantic retrieval.

> **Key Insight:** Embeddings convert language into geometry. Once meaning lives in vector space, "find the most relevant document" becomes "find the nearest neighbor" â€” and that single idea is the engine behind semantic search, recommendation, clustering, and every RAG system you'll ever build.

animation_sequence.title: From Words to Vectors
animation_sequence.body: Vaathiyaar reveals the secret behind modern AI search: every sentence can become a point in a 384-dimensional space, where closeness means similar meaning. Meet all-MiniLM-L6-v2 â€” tiny, fast, and the default brain of local RAG.

steps.explanation: Import the library. SentenceTransformer loads models; util has similarity helpers.
Download (once) and load the lightweight 80 MB model. It runs offline on CPU.

steps.code:
docs = [
    'Reset your password from Settings.',
    'Our office opens at 9 AM weekdays.',
    'Contact billing for refund requests.',
]
steps.explanation: Our tiny knowledge base â€” the documents we want to make searchable.
Embed every document into a 384-dim vector. Do this once and reuse it.

steps.code: query = 'I cannot log into my account'
steps.explanation: A user's question â€” phrased with NO keywords from the password document.
Embed the query into the same vector space as the documents.
Find the nearest document by cosine similarity â€” the core retrieval step of RAG.
Extract the winning document using the returned corpus index.

steps.code:
print(best)
# -> Reset your password from Settings.
steps.explanation: Semantic match! Meaning beat keywords â€” exactly what powers RAG retrieval.

animation_sequence.title: You Built Semantic Search!
animation_sequence.body: You just turned language into geometry and retrieved by meaning. This is the retrieval half of every RAG system. XP awarded!

instruction.en: Complete the function `most_similar(model, query, docs)` so it returns the single document from `docs` whose meaning is closest to `query`. Use the model to encode both the query and the documents, then use `util.semantic_search` (or cosine similarity) to find the top match.

practice_challenges.starter_code:
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer, util

def most_similar(model, query, docs):
    # 1. Encode docs into tensors
    # 2. Encode the query into a tensor
    # 3. Use util.semantic_search with top_k=1
    # 4. Return the best-matching document string
    pass

if __name__ == '__main__':
    model = SentenceTransformer('all-MiniLM-L6-v2')
    docs = [
        'Reset your password from the Settings page.',
        'Our office opens at 9 AM on weekdays.',
        'Contact billing for refund requests.',
    ]
    print(most_similar(model, 'I forgot my login', docs))
practice_challenges.expected_output: Reset your password from the Settings page.

en: Encode documents once with model.encode(docs, convert_to_tensor=True).
util.semantic_search(query_emb, doc_emb, top_k=1) returns a list of lists of hit dicts.
The matching document index is hits[0][0]['corpus_id'] â€” use it to index into docs.

practice_challenges.test_code:
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer

model = SentenceTransformer('all-MiniLM-L6-v2')
docs = [
    'Reset your password from the Settings page.',
    'Our office opens at 9 AM on weekdays.',
    'Contact billing for refund requests.',
]
assert most_similar(model, 'I forgot my login', docs) == 'Reset your password from the Settings page.'
assert most_similar(model, 'When can I visit you?', docs) == 'Our office opens at 9 AM on weekdays.'
assert most_similar(model, 'I want my money back', docs) == 'Contact billing for refund requests.'

instruction.en: Write a function `similarity_score(model, text_a, text_b)` that returns the cosine similarity between two sentences as a Python float rounded to 2 decimals. Embed both sentences and use util.cos_sim.

practice_challenges.starter_code:
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer, util

def similarity_score(model, text_a, text_b):
    # Encode both texts (convert_to_tensor=True)
    # Compute util.cos_sim and return a rounded float
    pass

if __name__ == '__main__':
    model = SentenceTransformer('all-MiniLM-L6-v2')
    print(similarity_score(model, 'How do I reset my password?', 'I forgot my login credentials'))

en: util.cos_sim returns a 2D tensor; grab the scalar with .item().
Unrelated sentences score near 0.0; near-paraphrases score above 0.4.

practice_challenges.test_code:
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer

model = SentenceTransformer('all-MiniLM-L6-v2')
high = similarity_score(model, 'How do I reset my password?', 'I forgot my login credentials')
low = similarity_score(model, 'How do I reset my password?', 'The weather is sunny today')
assert isinstance(high, float)
assert high > low
assert high > 0.3
assert low < 0.3
